For performance: use separate dashboards for different modes rather than conditional cards.

Home Assistant with the AWTRIX integration drives small LED matrix displays for live home status information. UNSAID: AWTRIX 3 (running on ESP32 with a 32x8 LED matrix) displays: current time/date, entity states, scrolling text, custom icons, and weather. The HA AWTRIX integration sends custom notifications and data to the display via MQTT. The display as a 'glanceable UI': show the home's status (alarm armed, washing machine running, next calendar event) on a small always-on display without needing a tablet. The custom icon capability: upload 8x8 pixel icons for any entity type. For families: display personalized messages — 'Welcome home, Alice!' when Alice's person entity changes to home. AWTRIX is an ESPHome-compatible alternative firmware for the same hardware.

Home Assistant's virtual thermostat (generic_thermostat) enables temperature control using a temperature sensor and a switch, converting any HVAC system into a HA-managed thermostat. UNSAID: generic_thermostat: heater: switch.furnace_relay sensor: sensor.living_room_temp target_sensor: sensor.living_room_temp min_temp: 15 max_temp: 25 ac_mode: false turns on the switch.furnace_relay when the temperature drops below the setpoint and off when it reaches the setpoint. This pattern enables: adding smart scheduling to dumb thermostats (just add a smart relay + temperature sensor); zone-based heating (separate thermostat per room with independent control); and integration with presence detection for away mode setbacks. The hysteresis (cold_tolerance, hot_tolerance) parameters prevent rapid relay cycling.

FAILURE: Assuming sun-based triggers (sunrise, sunset) fire at exactly the same time each day causes confusion when automations seem to shift over weeks. UNSAID: The sun trigger fires relative to actual astronomical sunrise/sunset, which shifts throughout the year (up to 3+ hours difference between winter and summer solstice at higher latitudes). An automation set to 'sunset - 30 minutes' fires at 4:00 PM in December but 8:30 PM in June. This is usually the desired behavior for exterior lights. The bug occurs when absolute-time automations are mixed with sun-relative automations without accounting for the shift. For indoor automations: use absolute time triggers. For exterior lighting: use sun-relative triggers. The time_pattern trigger: '*/15 * * * *' provides cron-like precision when needed.

Home Assistant with the Google Cast and Apple TV integrations enables media-aware home automations based on what's playing. UNSAID: The media_player entities from Google Cast (Chromecast, Nest Hub) and Apple TV expose: playing state, media title, app name, volume. Automation triggers: media_player turns on → dim lights; media_player starts playing → pause TTS announcements; media_player becomes idle → restore lights. The media control scene: 'Movie mode' automation triggered by Plex or Netflix app becoming active: dim all living room lights to 10%, close smart blinds, disable motion-triggered lights. The Apple TV integration via pyatv provides rich state including current app, play/pause, and can send remote control commands from HA.

Home Assistant's notify group and notification templates enable targeted, context-aware notifications to multiple recipients. UNSAID: notify.all_residents group: send to all household members simultaneously. Template-based notifications: data: message: 'The {{ trigger.to_state.attributes.friendly_name }} has been {{ trigger.to_state.state }}' produces 'The Front Door has been opened'. The ios.category feature on iPhone enables notification groupings and thread IDs. The push notification image: data: attachment: url: '{{ state_attr("camera.front_door", "entity_picture") }}' attaches the current camera snapshot. The critical alert: iOS users should configure the critical alert sound for safety notifications — these bypass silent mode.

Home Assistant automations run sequentially by default in the event loop — long-running automations can delay subsequent automation triggers. UNSAID: An automation with a 5-minute delay { wait: 300 } doesn't block the event loop — wait uses asyncio sleep. But actions that call blocking services (a synchronous custom component, or a shell command via command_line) block the event loop for their duration. Multiple simultaneously triggered automations that each have synchronous actions can cause a queue backlog visible as delayed state updates in the UI. The fix: ensure all custom component service calls are async (use hass.async_add_executor_job for blocking I/O); avoid long shell_command calls (use command_line sensor instead).

Home Assistant with Zigbee2MQTT and Aqara sensors provides the most cost-effective environmental monitoring solution. UNSAID: Aqara TVOC Air Quality Monitor, Temperature and Humidity sensors, door/window sensors, and cube controllers provide rich sensing data at $10-30 per device. Joined via Zigbee2MQTT: automatic entity creation with all sensor readings exposed. The TVOC sensor: total volatile organic compounds, CO2 equivalent, temperature, humidity in one device. Battery life: Aqara sensors typically last 1-2 years on a CR2032 battery. The Aqara cube: 6-side multiplex with rotation and shake triggers — 18 possible actions per cube for scene control without wall switches. Aqara's Matter support (2024+) enables multi-platform compatibility alongside Zigbee.

Home Assistant's entity platform covers all device types through standardized entity classes, enabling consistent control across brands. UNSAID: The entity platforms: light (on/off, brightness, color), climate (thermostat, AC, heat pump), cover (blinds, garage doors, awnings), media_player (speakers, TVs, streaming devices), vacuum (robot vacuums), sensor, binary_sensor, switch, lock, alarm_control_panel, button (stateless), event (for momentary inputs), number (setpoints), select (dropdown settings), text (string input), update (firmware notifications). Understanding the platform determines what services are available: a cover entity uses cover.open/close/stop instead of homeassistant.turn_on. Always use the platform-specific services for device control, not generic homeassistant.turn_on.

FAILURE: Not version-controlling Home Assistant configuration prevents recovery from configuration mistakes and loses automation history. UNSAID: Without git for HA config, a mistaken automation deletion or configuration.yaml edit that breaks HA has no rollback path. The git-based workflow: git init in /config; .gitignore for secrets.yaml and .storage/ (UI-created entities); commit after each change. The Studio Code Server addon (running in HA OS) provides a browser-based VS Code with git integration. GitHub Actions can validate configuration on push. The disaster recovery scenario: HA won't start after a bad configuration change; with git, git revert to the last working commit; without git, manual reconstruction from memory. Version control is non-optional infrastructure for any HA installation that took more than 10 hours to configure.

Home Assistant with the Studio Code Server addon provides browser-based YAML editing with syntax highlighting, validation, and git integration directly in the HA interface. UNSAID: Studio Code Server runs VS Code in a browser tab within HA OS, replacing the need for SSH + vim/nano for configuration editing. The HA YAML extension provides: schema validation (catches unknown keys, wrong value types), autocompletion for service names and entity IDs, and integration-specific documentation on hover. The git integration: changes to config files are tracked in VS Code's source control panel. The remote access: Studio Code Server is accessible from outside the home via Nabu Casa/VPN, enabling configuration editing from anywhere. This addon is the recommended development environment for HA power users.

Home Assistant's integration with solar inverters (Enphase, SolarEdge, SMA, Fronius) enables solar-aware automation of high-consumption appliances. UNSAID: Solar integrations provide: current generation (Watts), daily production (kWh), battery state of charge (for storage systems), and grid import/export data. Solar-aware automation: when solar production exceeds 2000W AND battery is above 80% → start dishwasher, heat hot water tank, charge EV (in priority order). This maximizes self-consumption of solar energy. The Nordpool or EnergyZero integrations provide electricity spot prices — combine with solar data for cost-optimal automation: heat the water tank when solar is producing OR when electricity price is below €0.05/kWh.

Home Assistant's Companion App location tracking drains phone battery significantly when set to high accuracy. UNSAID: The HA Companion App's background location tracking uses GPS, which is battery-intensive. The significant location changes mode uses the phone's network/cell tower tracking (lower accuracy, less battery drain) and only updates when the phone moves significantly. For presence detection (home/away only): zone tracking with significant changes is adequate and much more battery-friendly. For precise location (GPS coordinates, multiple zone tracking): high accuracy with ~20% additional daily battery drain. The background processing setting on iOS requires 'Always Allow' location permission — users often change to 'While Using' which breaks background tracking.

Home Assistant with CO2, air quality, and radon sensors enables health-based ventilation automation. UNSAID: CO2 above 1000ppm impairs cognitive function; above 2000ppm causes headaches. The automation: Aranet4 or SCD30 CO2 sensor above 1000ppm → open motorized window or activate heat recovery ventilation. Radon monitors (RadonEye RD200 via Bluetooth LE): long-term radon exposure is the second leading cause of lung cancer. HA radon monitoring: alert if 3-day average exceeds 100 Bq/m³. PM2.5 air quality: Ikea Vindstyrka or Aqara TVOC monitor → close windows when outdoor AQI is high; open when indoor PM2.5 exceeds outdoor. Health-based ventilation automation prevents the set-and-forget problem of manual ventilation schedules.

Home Assistant's firmware update management for all integrated devices provides a centralized view of available updates across manufacturers. UNSAID: The Update integration exposes firmware update notifications for devices that support OTA updates: ESPHome devices, Zigbee2MQTT devices (via OTA), smart speakers, and some WiFi devices. The update entity has: installed_version, latest_version, update_percentage. HA can trigger firmware updates for ESPHome devices via the update.install service. The monthly maintenance automation: on the first Saturday of the month → check all update entities → send notification listing available updates → optionally auto-update low-risk devices (ESPHome, Zigbee2MQTT). Centralizing firmware updates prevents security vulnerabilities from accumulating across dozens of devices.

Home Assistant's monthly release cadence provides fast innovation but requires active update management to prevent integration breakage. UNSAID: HA releases a new version on the first Wednesday of each month with new features, breaking changes, and integration updates. The risk: each update may deprecate APIs used by HACS custom components or change entity attributes that automations depend on. The update strategy: read the release notes and breaking changes before updating (subscribed via RSS or the HA blog); backup before updating; update within 1-2 months of release to stay current with security fixes. The critical window: if a security vulnerability is fixed, update within 1 week; for feature updates, 2-4 weeks is acceptable. Never be more than 3 major versions behind (3 months) to avoid accumulating breaking changes.

Home Assistant integration with electric vehicle chargers (OCPP protocol, Wallbox, Zappi) enables solar-optimized and time-of-use-rate EV charging. UNSAID: The OCPP integration (community) supports generic OCPP-compatible chargers. Wallbox and myenergi Zappi have dedicated integrations. The solar-optimized charging automation: set charge rate to solar surplus power; minimum 6A (required for Type 2 charging); adjust every 5 minutes based on current solar output. Time-of-use optimization: charge at the cheapest electricity rate time (typically 2-4am for off-peak tariffs); stop charging before peak rate times. Combined: prefer solar charging during the day; fall back to off-peak grid if insufficient solar. This automation can save $500-2000/year on EV charging costs depending on electricity prices.

FAILURE: Writing HA automations that assume all entities are available on startup causes errors during HA boot. UNSAID: HA takes 30-120 seconds to start and entities become available gradually as integrations initialize. An automation triggered by homeassistant.start that immediately calls a service on an entity that hasn't initialized yet fails silently or produces 'entity not found' errors. The fix: add a short delay or use wait_template to confirm entities are available: wait_template: "{{ not is_state('light.living_room', 'unavailable') }}". The homeassistant_started event fires after the full boot (all integrations loaded), while homeassistant_started_fast fires earlier. For startup automations: use homeassistant_started (not start), and add entity availability checks.

Home Assistant's Bluetooth integration scans for BLE devices, enabling presence detection via Bluetooth beacons and integration with BLE sensors. UNSAID: The Bluetooth integration (HA 2022.8+) runs Bluetooth scanning in the background, discovering BLE advertisements from nearby devices. Bluetooth proxy: ESPHome devices configured as Bluetooth proxies extend BLE range throughout the home — crucial for covering large homes beyond the HA host's Bluetooth range. BLE presence: iBeacon tiles, Apple AirTags (limited), or custom ESPHome beacons provide room-level presence detection. BLE sensors: Govee temperature sensors, Xiaomi MiFlora plant sensors, and many health devices expose data via BLE advertisements read by HA. The BLE proxy network can cover a 3000 sq ft home with 3-4 ESPHome proxy devices.

Home Assistant add-ons are only available for HA OS and HA Supervised installations — not for HA Container or HA Core. UNSAID: The HA installation types: HA OS (full, recommended — add-ons available); HA Supervised (HA on any Linux, add-ons available but unsupported); HA Container (HA in Docker, NO add-ons — run additional containers separately); HA Core (Python venv, NO add-ons). Many popular features (Mosquitto, Zigbee2MQTT, Frigate, Studio Code Server) are distributed as add-ons. HA Container users must run these as separate Docker containers and connect them via network — more operational complexity but more control over the hosting environment. The recommendation: HA OS for most users; HA Container for advanced users who already manage Docker infrastructure.

Home Assistant with the OpenAI Conversation or local Ollama integration enables natural language queries about home state. UNSAID: The extended openai_conversation integration (HACS) sends HA entity states and services to the OpenAI API as context, enabling: 'Is anyone home?', 'What temperature is the bedroom?', 'Turn on all outdoor lights'. The local alternative: Ollama running llama3.1 or mistral provides AI responses without API costs or data leaving the home. The function calling capability: the AI calls HA services (light.turn_on, climate.set_temperature) based on the natural language command. The practical limitation: language models occasionally hallucinate entity names or call incorrect services — review AI action proposals for critical operations. The integration is best for exploratory queries and simple control, not complex multi-step automations.

Home Assistant virtual buttons (button platform) provide stateless triggers for one-shot actions callable from dashboards, automations, or voice. UNSAID: input_button creates a momentary button entity that fires an event when pressed but has no persistent state (unlike input_boolean). The event trigger: platform: event event_type: call_service event_data: service: input_button.press entity_id: input_button.morning_routine. The use case: a 'Morning Routine' button on the dashboard that triggers a complex script. Button entities also appear in the companion app dashboard for voice assistant integration ('Hey Siri, activate morning routine' via the HA Siri Shortcuts). The difference from input_boolean: a button can't be 'on' or 'off' — it only fires press events, making it semantically correct for trigger actions.

FAILURE: Not monitoring HA system health metrics causes undetected degradation until a user-visible failure occurs. UNSAID: HA includes built-in system health sensors (Settings → System → Repairs): recorder database size, last recorder write, integration error counts, and network latency. The proactive monitoring automation: daily check of database size → alert if growing faster than expected; weekly check of integration errors → notify if any integration has error count > 0. The HA System Health integration exposes these metrics as sensors. Undetected problems: a cloud integration silently failing for weeks means automation data is stale; a growing database that causes slowdowns could have been caught earlier. Treat HA as production infrastructure requiring active health monitoring.

Home Assistant with the Roborock or Dreame vacuum integration enables context-aware cleaning automation triggered by presence and schedule. UNSAID: The vacuum integration exposes: start/stop/pause/return_to_base services, room-level cleaning (clean specific rooms), status (cleaning, returning, idle, docked, error), battery level, and map data. The presence-based cleaning automation: all persons leave home AND vacuum is docked AND last cleaning was more than 23 hours ago → start cleaning. The zone cleaning pattern: after a dinner party event (calendar trigger) → clean only kitchen and dining room. The notification pattern: vacuum has an error → push notification with error details and 'clear error' action button. Map card (HACS: Xiaomi Cloud Map Extractor or similar) shows the robot's current position on the floor plan.

Home Assistant's history_stats sensor provides counted statistics over time windows: how long was the light on today, how many times did the door open this week. UNSAID: sensor: - platform: history_stats name: Front Door Today entity_id: binary_sensor.front_door type: count start: '{{ now().replace(hour=0, minute=0, second=0) }}' end: '{{ now() }}' counts how many times the front door opened today. The type options: time (duration in hours), ratio (percentage of time), count (number of state changes). Use cases: daily step counts from a door sensor (workout tracker), weekly appliance runtime tracking for maintenance, room occupancy duration for HVAC efficiency analysis. These statistics enable understanding of home usage patterns beyond simple state queries.

FAILURE: Using automations with restart mode for long-running sequences causes incomplete action execution when re-triggered. UNSAID: mode: restart cancels the current automation run and starts fresh. For a bedtime automation (dim lights over 10 minutes, lock doors, set HVAC) that uses restart mode: if someone triggers it twice within 10 minutes, the second trigger cancels the first midway — lights may be half-dimmed, doors unlocked, HVAC not set. For idempotent sequences: use mode: single (skip if already running) or mode: queued (run sequentially without cancellation). Reserve mode: restart for automations where re-trigger should override current state (motion lights where each motion extends the timeout). Document the mode choice with a comment explaining why.

Home Assistant with the DSMR/P1 integration reads smart electricity and gas meters directly via P1 port, providing real-time energy data without third-party services. UNSAID: Dutch, Belgian, and some European smart meters have a P1 data port outputting DSMR telegrams. A P1-to-USB cable ($15) or P1-to-WiFi dongle (Homewizard, SLIMMELEZER) connects the meter to HA. Data provided: current electricity import/export power (Watts), cumulative import/export (kWh), current gas consumption, and tariff indicators. This data feeds directly into HA's Energy Dashboard with sub-second resolution. The local advantage: data stays in the home; no subscription required; more frequent updates than third-party energy apps. Outside Netherlands: Homewizard Energy P1 supports Dutch/Belgian meters; other countries have similar standards (SML protocol for Germany).

Home Assistant's template integration with trigger-based template sensors provides more efficient updating than state-reactive templates. UNSAID: Standard template sensors recalculate whenever any referenced entity changes. Trigger-based template sensors only update when a specific trigger fires: trigger: - platform: time_pattern minutes: /5 means the sensor only recalculates every 5 minutes regardless of entity changes. This is dramatically more efficient for derived sensors that don't need real-time updates (daily statistics, hourly summaries, battery status aggregates). The state: field is the primary value; attributes: can add multiple values; availability: defines the unavailable condition. Trigger-based templates support all trigger types including state, numeric_state, time, event, and zone.

Home Assistant's YAML automation IDs are required for the automation editor to function properly and for automation traces to persist across restarts. UNSAID: Without id: fields in automation YAML, the automation editor can't link UI changes to YAML entries, causing the UI to create duplicate automations on save. The trace feature stores traces keyed by automation ID — automations without IDs lose traces on HA restart. The id format: any unique string (common: slugified automation name, date-prefixed, or UUID). automations.yaml generated by the UI always includes IDs. Manual YAML automations: always add id: manually. The id: field has no impact on functionality — it only enables the UI integration and trace persistence. Best practice: add ids to all automations, even manually-written ones.

Home Assistant with the Govee integration and LED light strips provides affordable smart ambient lighting with local BLE control. UNSAID: Govee LED strips ($20-50) provide RGBIC (individually addressable zones) and music sync capabilities. The Govee integration via HACS supports local BLE control for newer Govee devices (eliminating cloud dependency). The HA light entity exposes full color control via light.turn_on with rgb_color, brightness, and effect parameters. Govee scenes (built-in effects like 'Sunrise', 'Candle Flicker') are accessible via the effect service. The limitation: Govee's BLE protocol isn't fully reverse-engineered for all models — some effects and features require the cloud API. For local-first lighting: WLED on custom LED strips provides more complete local control.

Home Assistant's notify.persistent_notification creates dashboard alerts that persist until dismissed, appropriate for non-urgent but important notices. UNSAID: persistent_notification.create with title and message creates a notification in the HA UI notification tray (bell icon). The notification_id parameter allows updating or dismissing specific notifications programmatically. The automation pattern: detect a condition (smoke detector battery low, water leak sensor activated) → create persistent notification AND send mobile push → when condition clears → dismiss the persistent notification. The two-part pattern: push notification for immediate awareness; persistent notification as a reminder until the issue is resolved. This prevents the 'I got the alert but forgot to fix it' problem — the persistent notification stays visible until action is taken.

FAILURE: Zigbee mesh degradation from interference or missing routers causes intermittent device failures that are hard to diagnose. UNSAID: Zigbee mesh quality degrades when: USB 3.0 devices near the coordinator emit 2.4GHz interference (use USB 2.0 extension cables); router nodes (mains-powered devices) are too far apart; smart bulbs reset to router mode on power interruption. The symptoms: devices randomly go unavailable for minutes, automations that 'sometimes don't work', new device join failures. The diagnosis: Zigbee2MQTT network map shows signal strength (LQI values) and routing paths — LQI below 50 indicates poor connection. The fix: add mains-powered router nodes in coverage gaps; use a 1-meter USB extension cable to move the coordinator away from the computer.

Home Assistant with the SpaceAPI or custom webhook integration connects to external systems, enabling corporate calendar-based home automation. UNSAID: Webhooks (HA's webhook trigger or webhook_id on triggers) accept incoming HTTP POST requests from external services. Use cases: doorbell app sends webhook to HA when visitor arrives; GitHub Actions sends webhook when deployment completes → flash lights; corporate calendar webhook (via Zapier or Make.com) sends work meeting start events → activate 'on a call' mode (DND on smart displays, red occupancy indicator light, mute smart speakers). The HA webhook URL: https://your-ha-instance/api/webhook/{webhook_id}. Securing webhooks: the webhook_id acts as a token — keep it private; for additional security, validate the payload signature if the sending service supports it.

Home Assistant's target area selector in service calls enables room-based control that scales with the home layout without hardcoding entity lists. UNSAID: service: light.turn_off target: area_id: kitchen turns off ALL lights in the kitchen area — no need to enumerate each light entity. As new lights are added to the kitchen area, they're automatically included in this service call. The area selector also works in automations: if a motion sensor is assigned to the 'Hallway' area, turning on lights in the 'Hallway' area automatically includes all lights in that area. This pattern is superior to maintaining entity lists in automations — adding a new device to an area automatically integrates it with existing automations.

Home Assistant stores UI-created entities (.storage/ directory) and configuration.yaml entities separately, and they don't automatically synchronize. UNSAID: Helpers created via the UI (Settings → Helpers → Add Helper) are stored in .storage/core.config_entries and similar JSON files — NOT in configuration.yaml. YAML-defined helpers (input_boolean: section in configuration.yaml) are separate from UI-created helpers. A YAML-defined helper and a UI-created helper with the same name will BOTH exist as separate entities with different entity_ids. The disaster: deleting .storage/ (a common 'fix' for corruption) deletes ALL UI-created entities, dashboards, automations created in the UI, user accounts, and integration configurations. Never delete .storage/ — always restore from backup if corrupted.

Home Assistant with the OpenHAB migration path: HA has become the dominant home automation platform and most OpenHAB users migrating to HA find better device support and community resources. UNSAID: OpenHAB's rule engine (Java DSL or Blockly) is more powerful than HA's YAML for complex automation logic but has a steeper learning curve. The migration strategy: identify all OpenHAB items (devices) and find equivalent HA integrations; recreate rules as HA automations (simple rules) or AppDaemon scripts (complex rules); use HA's MQTT integration to bridge devices that only work with OpenHAB bindings during transition. The practical reality: 80% of home automation use cases are simpler in HA; for the remaining 20% requiring complex logic, AppDaemon provides equivalent power to OpenHAB rules with Python.

Home Assistant's sensor state class and device class metadata enables proper unit handling, statistics, and dashboard display. UNSAID: state_class: measurement means the sensor tracks current values (temperature, power); state_class: total_increasing means the sensor accumulates (energy kWh meter) and HA uses it for statistics. device_class: energy tells HA to use this sensor in the Energy Dashboard. Without these metadata: sensor values appear correctly but don't integrate into HA's statistics engine or Energy Dashboard. The unit_of_measurement field must match the device class expectations — device_class: temperature with unit °C vs °F affects how HA converts and displays values. Custom integration developers must set these correctly; without them, the integration quality scale rates lower.

FAILURE: Creating automations with hardcoded entity_ids instead of using area or device targeting creates maintenance burden as entities are renamed or replaced. UNSAID: An automation listing 15 specific light entity_ids must be manually updated every time a light is replaced with a different brand (new entity_id). Using area_id targeting: updating which entities are in an area is done once in the device registry, not in every automation. The maintenance multiplier: 20 automations × 15 hardcoded entity_ids = 300 entity references to update when renovating a room. With area targeting: 20 automations with area_id: living_room automatically include new entities when they're assigned to the area. Design principle: automate to areas and groups, not to specific entity_ids, wherever possible.

Home Assistant with the Reolink integration provides local RTSP camera access with motion detection events without Reolink cloud subscription. UNSAID: Reolink cameras support local RTSP streams accessible directly on the LAN — enabling Frigate or direct RTSP integration in HA. The HA Reolink integration (2023+) provides: motion binary sensors, camera entity for live view, PTZ control for supported models, and smart detection (person/vehicle detection using Reolink's local AI). The local control: all features work without the Reolink app or cloud — just LAN access. Camera placement for HA integration: ensure cameras are on the camera VLAN with firewall rules allowing HA to access port 554 (RTSP). The privacy consideration: local camera feeds never leave the home network; cloud-subscription cameras send footage to third-party servers.

Home Assistant integration with Hue, Lifx, and Nanoleaf provides the richest smart lighting experience with full effect and scene capabilities. UNSAID: Philips Hue via local API (Hue Bridge): supports scenes (including dynamic gradient scenes), entertainment zones, and accessories (Hue Motion Sensor, Hue Dimmer). The Hue integration uses the local Bridge API — no cloud dependency. Lifx via local UDP protocol: per-bulb RGBW with infrared for night vision camera illumination; Lifx Beam/Tile for geometric light panels. Nanoleaf via local API: light panels with touch sensitivity and rhythm (music sync). The HA integration challenge: each ecosystem has unique capabilities not exposed through standard light entity — use the integration-specific services for advanced features. The universal light control (brightness, color) works consistently; effects and scenes are brand-specific.

Home Assistant's automation trigger firing behavior with multiple triggers requires understanding which trigger fired to implement correct logic. UNSAID: An automation with two triggers (sunrise and sunset) runs the same action sequence for both. To differentiate: use choose with condition: condition: template value_template: "{{ trigger.platform == 'sun' and trigger.event == 'sunrise' }}" for the sunrise branch. The trigger object contains: platform (the trigger type), entity_id (for state triggers), from_state, to_state, for (duration that was met), and event-specific data. In template strings within actions: {{ trigger.to_state.state }} accesses the new state of the triggering entity. Without accessing trigger context, multi-trigger automations can't branch based on which trigger fired.

Home Assistant with the Passive BLE Monitor integration reads BLE advertisement data from Xiaomi, Govee, and other sensors without pairing. UNSAID: Many BLE sensors broadcast their data (temperature, humidity, CO2) in BLE advertisement packets without requiring a connection/pairing. Passive BLE Monitor reads these broadcasts, decoding the Xiaomi MiBeacon, BTHome, and other formats. The privacy implication: these sensors broadcast their data to all BLE scanners in range — neighbors could theoretically read your home sensor data. The encryption option: some Xiaomi devices support encrypted broadcasts — Passive BLE Monitor supports the decryption key. The sensor coverage: Xiaomi LYWSD03MMC ($5 temperature/humidity sensor) is the most popular supported device, providing excellent accuracy at minimal cost.

Home Assistant automation with the 'last changed' attribute enables time-since-last-event logic without external state tracking. UNSAID: entity.last_changed is an ISO timestamp of when the entity last changed state. Template: {{ (now() - states.binary_sensor.motion.last_changed).total_seconds() / 60 > 30 }} is true if motion was last detected more than 30 minutes ago. This enables: 'has the door been open for more than 5 minutes?' without maintaining a separate timer; 'was the motion sensor last active more than 1 hour ago?' as a leave-detection confirmation. The gotcha: last_changed is in UTC, but now() is timezone-aware — the subtraction works correctly because Python handles timezone-aware datetime arithmetic automatically.

FAILURE: Using local push integrations (mDNS-based) without ensuring proper mDNS resolution causes devices to randomly disconnect. UNSAID: Local integrations like Philips Hue (local API), ESPHome, and Shelly use mDNS for device discovery and connection. On networks with multiple VLANs or misconfigured mDNS, devices registered with mDNS names (hue-bridge.local, kitchen-switch.local) fail to resolve because mDNS doesn't cross VLAN boundaries without an mDNS reflector. The fix: configure mDNS reflector on the router (Unifi, OpenWrt support this natively); or use static IP addresses for critical devices and configure integrations with IP addresses instead of hostnames. For VLAN-separated IoT networks: mDNS reflector is essential for HA to discover and connect to IoT devices on the IoT VLAN.

Home Assistant with a whole-home occupancy model based on multiple sensors provides more reliable automation than single-sensor presence detection. UNSAID: The occupancy model approach: each room has a binary sensor that's 'on' if anyone is in that room (based on: motion sensor, light switch activity, media player playing, CO2 rising, TV remote used). The room occupancy binary sensor uses a timer — remains 'on' for 10 minutes after last activity. The home occupancy sensor: house is occupied if any room is occupied OR any person entity is home. This layered model handles: sleeping occupants (no motion, but person present), multiple occupants, slow movers (elderly, sleeping children). The result: automations can target 'occupied rooms' rather than specific rooms, enabling truly smart lighting.

Home Assistant's integration ecosystem of 3000+ integrations makes it both the most capable and most complex smart home platform — the complexity is a direct consequence of the capability. UNSAID: Each integration is a Python module maintained by the community or HA team. The surface area: 3000 integrations × configuration options × entity types = enormous documentation and maintenance burden. HA manages this through: quality scale enforcement, integration health scores, breaking change deprecation periods, and the integration review process. The user implication: HA can control virtually any smart device from any manufacturer — but the depth of support varies from 'cloud API polling' (basic) to 'local push with energy monitoring' (excellent). Understanding integration quality before purchasing devices prevents buying unsupported or poorly-supported hardware.

Home Assistant with the Sonoff NSPanel Pro provides an all-in-one wall panel with touchscreen, Zigbee coordinator, and HA integration running HASP (HA Smart Panel) firmware. UNSAID: The NSPanel Pro (ESP32-based touchscreen wall panel) can run a custom firmware that exposes it as a full HA dashboard display, Zigbee coordinator, and physical switch. HA integration: the panel shows the room's entity states, allows direct control, and acts as a Zigbee coordinator for nearby devices. The advantage: replaces a wall switch, a Zigbee dongle, and a tablet display in one device. The installation: requires custom firmware (Squareline Studio-designed UI + ESPHome backend). The production-readiness: suitable for tech-savvy users comfortable with ESP32 firmware flashing; not recommended for non-technical household members as primary controls.

FAILURE: Not testing automations with 'trace' enabled leads to mystery bugs that only surface at night, on vacation, or during edge conditions. UNSAID: HA automation traces capture the full execution path including: which trigger fired, each condition evaluation result (pass/fail with reason), each action executed, and any errors. Without traces, debugging 'why didn't the bedroom automation run last Tuesday?' is impossible. The trace workflow: enable tracing on critical automations (traces: stored_traces: 20 in automation config); after a suspected failure, check the trace for that automation — it shows exactly what happened. The Developer Tools → Automations → (automation) → Traces view shows the last N traces. For periodic automations: trigger manually and verify the trace shows the expected execution path before trusting the automation in production.

Home Assistant with the Unifi integration provides network awareness of all devices on the home network, enabling presence detection and network health monitoring. UNSAID: The UniFi integration connects to a UniFi controller (UDM, UCK, or self-hosted UniFi Network) and creates device_tracker entities for all connected network clients. Presence detection: phone connects to UniFi WiFi → device_tracker.alice_phone becomes home. The advantage over ping-based detection: UniFi knows immediately when a device connects (no polling delay). Additional features: force provision, block/unblock clients (parental controls via HA automation), bandwidth sensors per client. The network monitoring integration: alerts when network congestion exceeds threshold; detects new unknown devices on the network (security monitoring).

Home Assistant's config flow UI for integration setup doesn't always expose all configuration options available in YAML. UNSAID: Some integrations have advanced options only available in configuration.yaml (not in the config flow UI). For example: certain MQTT discovery settings, advanced Zigbee2MQTT options, and some recorder exclusion patterns must be configured in YAML. The HA team's goal: eventually all integration options exposed via config flow. Current state (2025): most options are in config flow, but niche configurations sometimes require YAML. The documentation approach: always check both the integration documentation and the GitHub integration source code for all available options — the source code is authoritative. For integrations added via config flow: re-configure options via Settings → Integrations → (integration) → Configure.

Home Assistant with the iRobot Roomba integration and zone-based cleaning creates presence-aware automated home cleaning. UNSAID: The Roomba integration (via the roomba library) provides: start/stop/pause/dock services, cleaning map sensors, battery level, error status, and cleaning history. The zone cleaning pattern: define cleaning segments via the iRobot app; HA triggers specific room cleaning via vacuum.send_command with the room segment IDs. The presence-aware pattern: everyone leaves home → wait 5 minutes (confirm departure) → start Roomba in all rooms → Roomba returns to dock when done → send notification 'Cleaning complete'. The recharge-and-resume feature: if Roomba runs out of battery during large cleaning, it docks, charges, and resumes — HA automation doesn't need to handle this manually.

FAILURE: Not handling device unavailability in automations causes automations to fail silently when devices go offline. UNSAID: An automation that calls light.turn_on for a device that's unavailable logs a warning but doesn't stop the automation — subsequent actions still run. The problem: an automation sequence that depends on a device being available (check door lock state → if locked, proceed) won't fail explicitly if the lock is unavailable, potentially proceeding with incorrect assumptions. The defensive pattern: add condition: condition: not conditions: - condition: state entity_id: lock.front_door state: unavailable to abort if the device is unavailable. The monitoring pattern: a template sensor counting unavailable security devices triggers an alert when any security device goes offline unexpectedly.

Home Assistant's TTS (Text-to-Speech) with the Piper integration provides high-quality, customizable offline speech synthesis. UNSAID: Piper TTS (running as a Wyoming protocol server) generates speech from text using neural TTS models, with 60+ language models available. Voice quality: Piper models range from 'low' (fast, acceptable quality) to 'high' (slow, near-human quality). The HA tts.piper service: speed, volume, and pitch adjustable per call. The announcement pattern: tts.speak service_data: entity_id: media_player.kitchen_speaker message: 'The {{ area_name(trigger.event.data.area_id) }} door has been left open for 5 minutes'. TTS enables natural language home status announcements without hardcoded messages. The kitchen/hallway speaker setup: ceiling speakers connected to a Chromecast Audio or DLNA streamer provide whole-home TTS.

Home Assistant's architecture decision to store all state in memory and sync to database (not query database for state) enables millisecond state access at the cost of memory usage. UNSAID: HA maintains a complete in-memory state machine — every entity's current state is in RAM. Database writes happen asynchronously for history/persistence; reads from the state machine (not the database) for current state. This design makes state access O(1) regardless of entity count — 1000 entities is as fast as 10. The trade-off: large HA installations with 1000+ entities require more RAM (2-4GB recommended). The alternative (database-as-state) would require a database round-trip for every service call and automation evaluation, making automation response 10-100x slower. Memory-resident state is the correct architecture for a real-time home automation platform.

Home Assistant with the Telegram bot integration provides secure remote control via messaging without exposing HA to the internet. UNSAID: The Telegram bot integration enables: sending messages from HA, receiving commands from users, displaying inline keyboards for quick actions, and sending photos from cameras. Security: the bot only accepts messages from whitelisted chat_ids — you must send a message to the bot first to discover your chat_id. Commands: /status returns a summary of home state; /lock runs the lock automation; /cameras sends snapshots from all cameras. The use case: secure access to HA functionality when away from home without opening any ports or configuring VPN. Response time: Telegram delivery is near-instant (< 1 second from HA command to received message).

Home Assistant's recorder database uses SQLite by default which has write performance limitations on Raspberry Pi's SD card / SSD. UNSAID: SQLite on a Raspberry Pi 4 SSD handles HA's typical write load (100-300 writes/second during peak automation activity) but struggles with concurrent queries during high entity count scenarios. The write amplification: each entity state change triggers: a state write, a state_attributes write (if attributes changed), and periodic long-term statistics writes. For 500+ entities with frequent updates: the recorder queue can grow during peak periods, adding latency to the entity state history. Solution: move to PostgreSQL (lower write amplification, better concurrency), increase purge_keep_days reduction, or add more indexes with DB tuning. The symptom of an overloaded recorder: 'last updated' timestamps in the UI lagging by 5-60 seconds behind actual changes.

Home Assistant with the Google Assistant and Amazon Alexa integrations via Nabu Casa enables voice control through commercial assistants while keeping core logic local. UNSAID: Nabu Casa's cloud connection exposes HA entities to Google Assistant and Amazon Alexa, enabling 'Hey Google, turn on the living room lights'. The HA logic runs locally — Nabu Casa only relays the voice command intent. Without Nabu Casa: self-hosted exposure via Nginx + domain + Google/Amazon OAuth requires significant configuration. The entity exposure: HA exposes entities to Google/Alexa by entity_id or area — exclude diagnostic sensors, internal helpers, and anything users shouldn't voice control. The naming convention: entity friendly names should be voice-friendly phrases ('Living Room Lamp' not 'light.lr_main_ceiling_1').

Home Assistant with the Frigate NVR and CodeProject AI provides more accurate person detection without Google Coral hardware. UNSAID: CodeProject AI runs CPU-based AI inference for object detection — slower than Coral TPU but doesn't require hardware purchase. For 1-3 cameras with 1080p at 5fps: CodeProject AI on a modern i5 CPU processes detection within 200-400ms (acceptable for most use cases). Integration: Frigate's custom detectors option connects to CodeProject AI. The detection models: YOLOv8 nano/small models provide a good accuracy/speed tradeoff. For privacy: CodeProject AI runs entirely locally — no detection data leaves the home. The accuracy improvement over CPU OpenCV: neural network-based detection has significantly fewer false positives (pets, shadows, leaves) compared to traditional motion detection.

FAILURE: Automations that interact with media players without checking player availability or current state cause unexpected interruptions. UNSAID: An automation that sets volume or plays a notification on a media player interrupts ongoing playback — a 3am automation playing a low-battery notification on the bedroom speaker wakes sleeping occupants. The defensive pattern: check the media player state before playing: condition: condition: not conditions: - condition: state entity_id: media_player.bedroom state: 'playing'. For critical alerts (smoke, water leak): override this check. For routine notifications: respect user activity. The announce: true feature (HA 2023.9+) handles this automatically for compatible players — it pauses current media, plays the announcement, then resumes.

Home Assistant's automation trigger on 'attribute change' enables fine-grained reactions to device state changes beyond on/off. UNSAID: trigger: platform: state entity_id: climate.living_room attribute: current_temperature to: react on attribute changes specifically. Without specifying attribute:, state triggers fire on any state or attribute change. The use case: trigger when the thermostat's current_temperature attribute changes to a specific value; react when a media player's media_title attribute changes (new song playing); alert when a battery_level attribute drops below 20%. Attribute-based triggers are more specific and efficient than template sensors that compute derived values from attributes and trigger on those computed changes.

Home Assistant complete smart home architecture for a modern home combines local protocols, edge AI, and energy management into a cohesive system. UNSAID: The recommended 2025 HA stack: HA OS on x86 mini PC (Intel N100) + SSD; Zigbee coordinator (Sonoff Zigbee 3.0 Dongle Plus) for sensors and switches; Z-Wave stick for locks and garage doors; ESPHome for custom sensors and DIY devices; Frigate + Coral TPU for camera AI; Piper TTS + OpenWakeWord for voice; Adaptive Lighting for circadian lighting; Alarmo for security system; energy management via P1 meter + solar inverter integration; InfluxDB + Grafana for analytics; Git + GitHub for config versioning; Nabu Casa for remote access and commercial assistant integration. This stack provides maximum functionality with full local control and no mandatory cloud subscriptions.

Home Assistant's template trigger type distinguishes between state, numeric_state, template, event, time_pattern, and webhook triggers by their evaluation model. UNSAID: Template triggers evaluate a Jinja2 expression that must transition from false to true — not just become true — meaning if HA restarts and the expression is already true, it will NOT fire until it first becomes false and then true again. This startup-skipping behavior catches many users who expect initialization triggers. Use state triggers for entity state changes, numeric_state for threshold crossings (with hysteresis via above/below simultaneously), and template for complex multi-entity conditions. The 'for:' parameter on state triggers creates a timer that restarts if the state changes back, preventing flicker triggers. For rate-limited sensors, use the 'value_template' on state triggers to filter noise without creating separate template entities.

Custom component development in Home Assistant follows a specific directory structure and async-first architecture. UNSAID: Place custom components in 'config/custom_components/<domain>/' with mandatory files: __init__.py (entry point with async_setup_entry), manifest.json (dependencies, version, requirements), and coordinator.py for API-polling components using DataUpdateCoordinator. The DataUpdateCoordinator pattern handles update scheduling, error propagation, and entity state updates atomically — avoiding the anti-pattern of each entity making independent API calls. For config flow UI, implement config_flow.py with HomeAssistantConfigFlow class. Use 'hacs.json' to publish via HACS. Critical: all I/O must be async or dispatched via hass.async_add_executor_job() to avoid blocking the event loop. Test with pytest-homeassistant-custom-component for unit tests and the HA dev container for integration testing.

HomeKit Bridge integration in Home Assistant exposes HA entities to Apple HomeKit ecosystem, enabling Siri voice control and Home app access without replacing the HA setup. UNSAID: Configure via Settings > Integrations and carefully filter which entities to expose — exposing all entities causes HomeKit pairing failure due to the 150-accessory limit per bridge. Create multiple HomeKit Bridge instances (up to 10) to segment accessories: one for lights, one for climate, one for security. Rename entities in HA before adding to HomeKit since names sync once and don't update automatically. The HomeKit Controller integration (separate from HomeKit Bridge) is for natively-HomeKit devices being added TO HA — a common source of confusion. For local control, HomeKit Bridge operates entirely on the local network with no cloud dependency, making it more reliable than many cloud-based voice platforms.

Smart appliance automation (washing machines, dishwashers, dryers) relies on power monitoring plugs with energy metering rather than dedicated smart appliances. UNSAID: The core pattern is a threshold-based state machine: idle (below 10W), running (above 50W), finishing (drops to 10-30W). Implement this with a helpers-based approach: a counter helper tracks consecutive low-power readings, and an automation fires when the count exceeds a threshold (3-5 readings at low wattage). This prevents false 'done' notifications from the brief power dips that occur mid-cycle. Use template sensors to abstract the state machine, then trigger notifications on state transition to 'idle.' Brands like Gosund EP2, IKEA Tradfri, and Shelly 2.5 support local energy monitoring. Cloud-based appliance integrations (Samsung SmartThings, LG ThinQ) are unreliable compared to this local power monitoring approach.

evidence_refs.description: Template integration for state machine sensors

statement: Water leak detection automation in Home Assistant combines leak sensors with valve actuators for fully automated flood prevention. UNSAID: Use Zigbee leak sensors (Aqara SJCGQ11LM or Sonoff SNZB-05) in all risk areas: under sinks, near water heater, behind dishwasher, by washing machine. The automation must have mode: 'single' to prevent duplicate executions, and should close the main water valve (Zigbee or Z-Wave ball valve like Dome DMWV1), send critical notifications, and log to logbook. Critical edge case: test valve closure quarterly because the valve motor can seize from disuse. For apartments, also automate stopping connected appliances (washing machine via smart plug power cutoff) to reduce water pressure even if no main valve exists. Battery monitoring for leak sensors is critical — a dead sensor gives false security.

Smart lock integration failures in Home Assistant commonly stem from Z-Wave lock quirks that cause the lock state to report incorrectly after manual use. UNSAID: Z-Wave locks (Yale, Schlage) report their state through 'lock' events that HA must poll or wait for. After manual key use or inside thumb-turn operation, the state update can take 30-120 seconds to propagate through Z-Wave to HA, creating a security gap where HA shows 'locked' while the door is actually unlocked. The fix is to combine a Z-Wave lock with a door contact sensor — the automation logic uses door sensor state + lock state together to determine true security state. For door codes, use the Z-Wave JS integration's lock code management service rather than direct MQTT, as it handles the proper Z-Wave User Code Command Class. Never rely solely on lock state for security-critical automations without corroborating sensor data.

evidence_refs.description: Z-Wave JS integration and lock code management

statement: Garage door automation in Home Assistant requires a tilt sensor on the door plus either a dedicated smart controller or a dry-contact relay. UNSAID: Commercial solutions like Ratgdo (ESPHome-based) or Meross MSG100 provide official HA integration with real-time state. DIY approach: a Shelly 1 relay connected to the garage opener's button terminals acts as a momentary switch (pulse duration 0.3-0.5s), while a Zigbee tilt sensor (Ecolink TILT-ZWAVE2.5-ECO) reports open/closed state. The critical insight is that garage openers are stateless toggles — a single relay activation toggles between open/close. If HA loses power while the door is transitioning, state becomes unknown. Add 'availability' checks in automations before triggering to avoid double-toggle issues. Implement a position timeout: if the door hasn't reached fully open/closed within 30s of triggering, send an alert about a possible obstruction.

evidence_refs.description: Cover entity for garage doors and gates

statement: NFC tag triggers in Home Assistant enable physical-world automation shortcuts that are more reliable than voice in noisy environments. UNSAID: NFC tags (NTAG213/215) are programmed via the HA companion app to fire tag_scanned events with the tag's unique ID. Unlike QR codes, NFC tags work without screen-on — a phone in pocket can scan a tag mounted at doorframe height. Common use cases: tag at front door entrance triggers 'arrive home' scene, tag at bedside triggers 'going to sleep' scene, tag in car triggers 'leaving home.' The tag scan event includes device_id of the scanning phone, enabling person-specific automation logic. Tags cost under $1 each. For Apple users, iOS Shortcuts can scan NFC and call HA webhooks as an alternative to the companion app flow. The limitation is NFC range (~4cm) making accidental triggers essentially impossible.

evidence_refs.description: NFC tags in Home Assistant blog post

statement: Schedule-based HVAC optimization in Home Assistant uses the Schedule helper to define weekly time blocks that feed into climate automations, separate from the thermostat's own schedule. UNSAID: Modern smart thermostats (ecobee, Nest via HA integration) have built-in scheduling, but overriding them from HA creates conflicts — the thermostat fights HA for control. The correct architecture: either use the thermostat's native schedule as authoritative, OR disable all thermostat schedules and manage everything from HA. Never split control between both. For generic_thermostat virtual thermostats, HA Schedule helpers work perfectly: define work/home/sleep blocks, use the schedule entity state in automation conditions to set temperature targets. The Climate entity's preset_mode service allows switching between 'home', 'away', 'sleep' modes directly, which is cleaner than setting absolute temperatures from automations.

Home Assistant Yellow and Green hardware platforms have specific peripheral limitations that affect integration capabilities. UNSAID: HA Yellow (CM4-based) includes a dedicated Zigbee/Thread/Matter radio (Silicon Labs EFR32MG21) that conflicts if you also plug a USB Zigbee coordinator — only one Zigbee coordinator can run per HA instance. HA Green lacks built-in radio, using USB stick for Zigbee/Z-Wave. Both platforms run on eMMC storage, which is faster and more reliable than SD cards but has a finite write endurance of ~3000 P/E cycles. High recorder polling rates (every 30s with many sensors) accelerate eMMC wear. The HA Yellow's M.2 slot supports NVMe SSDs for recorder/media storage while keeping the OS on eMMC — this split-storage approach extends hardware lifespan significantly. Thermal throttling occurs in enclosed spaces; ensure ventilation.

Community blueprints in Home Assistant allow importing pre-built automations that accept configuration inputs, reducing the need to build complex automations from scratch. UNSAID: Access blueprints via Settings > Automations > Blueprints > Import Blueprint, then paste a GitHub URL (raw content URL of a YAML file). Popular blueprint repos: home-assistant/core/tree/dev/homeassistant/components/blueprint, community blueprints on community.home-assistant.io. Blueprints use the 'blueprint:' key with 'input:' section defining configurable parameters (entities, durations, thresholds). The key limitation: blueprints cannot be edited after import via UI — to modify, export the resulting automation to YAML and convert it to a standalone automation. Always review blueprint code before importing; malicious blueprints can exfiltrate data via REST calls. The blueprint import process validates syntax but not semantic safety.

Motion-based lighting with adjustable color temperature uses the sun's position or time of day to shift light warmth throughout the day, supporting circadian rhythm alignment. UNSAID: The Adaptive Lighting integration (HACS) handles this automatically, but for manual implementation: create a template sensor that outputs color_temp in mireds (warm ~454 mireds/2200K at night, cool ~250 mireds/4000K at midday). Use the sun.sun entity's elevation attribute to interpolate between warm and cool values. The automation sets both brightness and color_temp when turning on lights, but only if a person is home and lights are already on — avoid waking sleeping people with unexpected light color changes. For Zigbee lights, color_temp support requires checking the light's color_mode capability first; some bulbs only support RGB without proper warm-white LED channels, making their 'warm white' mode an RGB approximation that looks different from real warm-white phosphor LEDs.

evidence_refs.description: Adaptive Lighting HACS integration for circadian rhythm

statement: Home Assistant long-term statistics corruption occurs when entity unit_of_measurement or device_class changes after statistics have been recorded, causing historical data to become incompatible with new unit interpretation. UNSAID: This commonly happens when updating integrations that change their sensor metadata. Symptoms: Energy dashboard showing incorrect totals, statistics graph showing unit mismatch warnings. The fix requires using Developer Tools > Statistics to manually remove bad statistics entries and resetting the statistic baseline. Alternatively, use the recorder purge service to clear all history and start fresh. Prevention: when migrating sensors (e.g., changing from a custom integration to a native one), keep the old entity_id OR manually migrate statistics via the homeassistant.update_entity_id service BEFORE the unit changes. The statistics tables in SQLite/PostgreSQL use numeric storage without unit metadata — unit interpretation comes solely from current entity metadata.

Home Assistant lovelace dashboard design follows a mobile-first card layout strategy because the companion app is the primary daily interaction surface. UNSAID: The default grid layout uses 4 columns on desktop and 1-2 on mobile, but Sections (new in 2024.3) provides a CSS grid-based layout that is responsive by default. Use Mushroom Cards (HACS) for visually consistent, compact cards that work well on mobile. The key information architecture principle: put the most-used controls on the first dashboard view, limit cards per view to 8-12 to avoid scrolling. Custom Sidebar (HACS) allows reordering the sidebar and hiding unused items. For multi-person households, create person-specific dashboard views with different tab icons. Dashboard YAML can be exported and version-controlled; storing it in a 'ui-lovelace.yaml' with 'lovelace:' in configuration.yaml gives full YAML control while disabling the UI editor for that dashboard.

Home Assistant's input helpers (input_boolean, input_number, input_select, input_datetime) persist state across restarts in .storage files but are NOT visible in the YAML-only configuration — they must be created via UI or configuration.yaml under the 'input_boolean:' key. UNSAID: The critical architectural distinction: helpers created via UI live in .storage/core.config_entries and are managed by the Storage collection; helpers defined in YAML live in configuration.yaml. Mixing both approaches causes confusion when helpers 'disappear' after a configuration reload that doesn't touch .storage. For production automations, prefer YAML-defined helpers for traceability and version control. Helpers created via UI can be exported to YAML via the Edit dialog (copy YAML button) then added to configuration.yaml. Counter and Timer helpers require 'counter:' and 'timer:' YAML keys respectively. Timer helpers are particularly useful for implementing cooldown periods in automations without custom scripts.

Multi-zone audio in Home Assistant using Snapcast or Music Assistant enables synchronized multi-room audio without Sonos pricing. UNSAID: Music Assistant (HACS) provides a unified media library and player control layer over Snapcast (synchronization engine), integrating with Spotify, local files, and online radio. The Snapcast server runs as a Docker container or HA add-on; Snapcast clients run on each speaker (Raspberry Pi Zero 2W + DAT hat = ~$25/zone). The HA Media Player entity exposes volume and play/pause controls that Music Assistant coordinates. Critical: Snapcast achieves synchronization via buffer management (typically 1-2 second latency), meaning it's unsuitable for video/movie audio sync but excellent for background music. Alternative: Volumio per-zone with HA integration provides per-zone library management. For simple setups, Chromecast Audio pucks (discontinued but available used) integrate natively with HA's Chromecast integration.

evidence_refs.description: Music Assistant documentation for multi-room audio

statement: Home Assistant automations that use 'wait_for_trigger' with a timeout can leave the automation in a zombie state if the HA process is restarted mid-wait. UNSAID: When HA restarts during a wait_for_trigger step, the automation's execution context is lost — the wait is abandoned and the automation never completes, with no error logged. This creates subtle bugs where expected follow-up actions (closing a valve, sending a completion notification) silently never happen. Mitigation: Use the 'continue_on_timeout: true' flag and handle the timeout case explicitly, logging a warning. For safety-critical wait sequences (water valve, security), implement a watchdog pattern: a separate automation monitors whether the expected state transition occurred within the window and alerts if not. Alternatively, restructure using automation state machines with input_boolean trackers that survive restarts, checking state on HA start.

evidence_refs.description: Wait for trigger script action documentation

statement: Home Assistant virtual device integration using MQTT discovery allows non-standard sensors (DIY Arduino, ESP32, custom hardware) to auto-register in HA without manual entity configuration. UNSAID: MQTT discovery works by publishing a JSON config payload to 'homeassistant/<component>/<object_id>/config' topic. The discovery message includes: name, unique_id, state_topic, unit_of_measurement, device_class, and device block (for grouping multiple sensors under one device card). After publishing, HA auto-creates the entity and subscribes to state_topic. For ESPHome devices this is automatic, but for custom firmware, implement the discovery publish in setup() and state updates in loop(). The unique_id field is critical — without it, entities can't be renamed or customized in HA UI. Use device MAC address as unique_id prefix to ensure uniqueness across flashed devices.

Home Assistant's event-driven architecture makes it fundamentally different from polling-based home automation systems, enabling near-instant reaction to state changes. UNSAID: HA's core is an event bus where all state changes fire events that automations can subscribe to. This means properly-integrated Zigbee devices (via Z2M or ZHA) trigger automations within 50-200ms of a button press, while polling-based integrations (cloud APIs polled every 30-60s) feel laggy. This is why local integrations are strongly preferred: cloud-dependent devices become second-class citizens in responsive automation. The event bus also enables advanced patterns: listen for multiple events in sequence (double-press = different action than single-press), use fire_event service calls to trigger synthetic events from scripts, and build inter-automation communication via custom events. The 'homeassistant_start' and 'homeassistant_stop' events enable graceful setup/teardown sequences.

evidence_refs.description: Home Assistant event architecture for developers

statement: Home Assistant integration with Cloudflare Tunnel provides secure remote access without port forwarding or VPN, replacing the paid HA Cloud for remote connectivity. UNSAID: Cloudflare Tunnel (formerly Argo Tunnel) creates an outbound-only encrypted tunnel from your HA instance to Cloudflare's edge, exposing it at a custom subdomain (e.g., ha.yourdomain.com). Setup: install cloudflared as Docker container or HA add-on, authenticate with Cloudflare account, configure tunnel to point to HA's internal port 8123. Add the Cloudflare domain to HA's 'trusted_proxies' list and set 'use_x_forwarded_for: true' in HTTP config. Critical security: enable Cloudflare Access (Zero Trust) in front of the tunnel to add Google/GitHub SSO as an authentication layer before even reaching HA's login page. Without Cloudflare Access, your HA login page is publicly accessible to brute-force attacks. Free tier sufficient for personal use. The companion app's external URL setting should point to the Cloudflare URL.

evidence_refs.description: Cloudflare Tunnel documentation for secure tunneling

statement: Home Assistant scene management follows a capture-and-apply pattern where scenes snapshot current entity states and can be recalled to restore them. UNSAID: Scenes in HA store a specific set of entity states (light brightness, color, switch state) but NOT dynamic values — they are static snapshots. Create scenes via UI (Settings > Areas > Scenes) or YAML. The key operational insight: use scene.apply service with transition time for smooth lighting changes instead of immediate switches. For 'movie mode' scenes, combine light scenes with media player state — but be aware that scene doesn't control what's playing, only device states. The homeassistant.snapshot_can_be_applied check lets you verify a scene is applicable before calling it. For complex multi-phase scenes (cinematic pre-movie dimming), chain scenes with delays in scripts rather than automations. Scenes can be exposed to Google Assistant/Alexa as virtual switches for voice-activated scene control.

Home Assistant Google Assistant and Amazon Alexa integration through HA Cloud (Nabu Casa) fails when entity names contain special characters or exceed character limits, causing unexposed devices. UNSAID: Voice assistant integrations have strict naming requirements: no parentheses, no symbols, max 30-character entity names for reliable recognition. Entities named 'Living Room Light (Main)' fail; 'Living Room Main Light' works. HA's Expose Entities screen (Settings > Voice Assistants) shows which entities are exposed, but doesn't warn about name compatibility issues. After renaming entities in HA, force a sync via the Google Home app or Alexa app — changes don't propagate automatically. The Nabu Casa subscription ($65/year) simplifies OAuth configuration versus the manual Google Cloud Console setup. Local voice processing via Wyoming + Whisper (HA add-ons) avoids these cloud naming issues entirely but requires more powerful hardware (Raspberry Pi 4 or better).

Home Assistant energy dashboard configuration requires proper sensor classification with state_class and device_class metadata for accurate consumption tracking. UNSAID: Energy dashboard only accepts sensors with state_class: 'total_increasing' (for cumulative energy meters) or state_class: 'total' (for meters that can decrease, like net metering). Sensors with state_class: 'measurement' (instantaneous power in Watts) are shown in the Power Flow card but NOT in energy consumption calculations. To add solar production: classify the solar sensor as device_class: 'energy' with state_class: 'total_increasing' and unit kWh. For grid-tied solar with net metering, you need separate 'grid import' and 'grid export' sensors. Shelly EM and SDM630 meters provide these separated values natively. For devices with only power (Watts) sensors but no energy accumulation, use the Riemann Sum integral helper to convert instantaneous power to cumulative energy — essential for DIY devices that don't have hardware energy metering.

Home Assistant add-ons run in separate Docker containers and cannot directly access the HA Python API — they communicate via the Supervisor API, MQTT, or WebSocket API only. UNSAID: This isolation is intentional for security, but it surprises developers who expect add-ons to have direct Python access. Add-ons that need HA integration must use the WebSocket API (similar to what the frontend uses) or MQTT. The Supervisor API provides add-on metadata, host access, and service discovery but NOT entity state access. For custom integrations that need both HA API access and external service hosting, the correct pattern is: custom_component (Python, direct API access) + add-on (Docker, service hosting) communicating via localhost MQTT or HTTP. AppDaemon is the exception: it's an add-on that wraps HA's Python API in its own runtime, providing Python callback-based automation without being a custom_component. The HACS-installed add-ons repository pattern requires the repository URL to be added to HACS's add-on repo list, not the integration list.

Home Assistant notification system design should use notification groups and severity levels to avoid companion app notification fatigue while ensuring critical alerts are never missed. UNSAID: Create a 'notify.all_devices' group combining all household members' companion apps. Use separate notification services for priority tiers: critical (smoke/CO/flood) uses notify with data.push.sound='default' and priority='high', routine (appliance done, package delivered) uses a persistent notification or low-priority push. iOS Critical Alerts bypass Do Not Disturb — use data.push.interruption-level='critical' ONLY for genuine emergencies. Implement a 'notification throttle' pattern using input_datetime helpers tracking last notification time per category, preventing duplicate alerts during flapping. For actionable notifications (lock/unlock door from notification), use action buttons in the notification payload. Android and iOS use different notification data schemas — test on both platforms when implementing custom notification features.

Home Assistant presence detection architecture combines multiple signal sources with a confidence-weighted state machine for reliable occupancy rather than relying on any single sensor. UNSAID: Single-source presence detection (WiFi device tracking, single motion sensor, single GPS) always has false-positive or false-negative failure modes. The proven architecture: Layer 1 (coarse, reliable) = phone GPS via companion app, updating only when significant location changes occur to preserve battery. Layer 2 (fine-grained) = home WiFi router device tracking for when/as phone enters/leaves local network. Layer 3 (room-level) = BLE beacons or Bluetooth RSSI via ESPHome devices. Combine layers: person is 'home' if GPS shows home zone AND WiFi sees device (or within 5 min of last WiFi presence). Room presence: BLE strongest signal among distributed BLE scanners. The Local Tuya/SwitchBot presence sensors that use mmWave radar (24GHz) are the emerging standard for room-level presence — no blind spots like PIR. Critical: integrate all sources into a single person entity rather than automating off raw device trackers.

evidence_refs.description: Person entity for unified presence detection

statement: Home Assistant's philosophy of local-first, privacy-respecting home automation positions it as fundamentally different from cloud-dependent ecosystems like Google Home or Amazon Alexa. UNSAID: The HA project explicitly optimizes for the scenario where the internet is unavailable and the user still needs reliable home control. This manifests in architectural decisions: integrations are penalized in quality score for cloud-only operation, the Matter protocol is embraced specifically because it's local-first, and Nabu Casa revenue subsidizes open-source development without creating a lock-in business model. The practical implication for users: every cloud-dependent device is a liability that will eventually have its cloud service discontinued (see Wink, Insteon, SmartThings developer platform changes). Building on Zigbee/Z-Wave/local API integrations creates a durable home automation stack. The 'Works with Home Assistant' certification program formalizes local API requirements for hardware partners.

evidence_refs.description: Works with Home Assistant certification program announcement

statement: Home Assistant database optimization for large deployments requires tuning the recorder configuration to balance historical data retention with query performance and storage usage. UNSAID: Default recorder settings record all entities every time they change, which creates database sizes of 1-10GB within months. Optimize with: 'purge_keep_days: 7' for most entities (sufficient for automation debugging), increased to 30-90 days for energy and climate sensors needed for trend analysis. Exclude high-frequency sensors (uptime counters, WiFi signal strength, CPU load) from recorder using the 'exclude:' config block — these sensors change every 30s and are never useful historically. For PostgreSQL: add indexes on 'states.entity_id' and 'events.event_type' columns if not present. Enable 'commit_interval: 1' (batches DB writes per second) instead of immediate commits. The Statistics tables (separate from States) use hourly/daily aggregation for long-term data — configure sensors to use statistics for historical graphs older than 30 days rather than raw state data.

Home Assistant Zigbee coordinator choice significantly impacts mesh stability; using a CC2531 USB dongle (obsolete) causes frequent device dropouts and limited routing capacity on networks larger than 15 devices. UNSAID: CC2531 is limited to 20 direct connections and lacks the processing power for large Zigbee meshes. Modern recommendations: Sonoff Zigbee 3.0 USB Dongle Plus (CC2652P) for networks up to 100 devices, or Elelabs ELU013 for enterprise-scale deployments. Flashing the coordinator with the latest firmware is critical — factory firmware often lags months behind with bug fixes. For Zigbee2MQTT, use the coordinator.backup feature before updating firmware. The physical placement of the coordinator matters enormously: USB3 interference from other USB3 devices causes 2.4GHz interference (Zigbee/WiFi/BLE all share this band). Use a USB extension cable (50-100cm) to move the dongle away from other USB devices. Switching coordinators requires re-pairing all devices or using the backup/restore feature.

evidence_refs.description: Zigbee2MQTT supported adapters and coordinator recommendations

statement: Home Assistant script vs automation design: scripts are reusable subroutines called from multiple automations, while automations contain the trigger logic that invokes scripts. UNSAID: A common anti-pattern is duplicating logic across multiple automations (morning routine triggered by weekday vs weekend schedules). The correct pattern: extract the shared logic into a script (e.g., 'script.morning_routine') with input variables for the specific variant, then have multiple automations call the same script with different variables. Scripts support 'fields:' parameter definitions with selectors for typed inputs. Scripts appear in the UI and can be manually triggered, making them also useful for one-tap control from dashboards. The architectural distinction: automations react to events/states; scripts execute sequences of actions. Both can call services, fire events, set variables, and invoke each other. For complex state machines, scripts with 'wait_template' and 'wait_for_trigger' provide sequential execution that automations' parallel modes can't replicate cleanly.

evidence_refs.description: Script integration for reusable action sequences

statement: Home Assistant WebSocket API has connection limits and message size constraints that affect custom integrations and third-party apps connecting simultaneously. UNSAID: HA's WebSocket API defaults allow up to 10 concurrent connections per user/token, with messages up to 512KB. Lovelace frontend, companion apps, Node-RED, AppDaemon, and custom scripts all compete for these connections. In large setups, running out of WebSocket connections causes intermittent dashboard loading failures without clear error messages. Increase limits via 'websocket_api: max_pending_tasks: 100' in configuration.yaml. Long-lived tokens for automation scripts should have limited scopes using user accounts with restricted permissions rather than admin tokens. The subscription model (subscribe_events, subscribe_trigger) should use specific event filters rather than subscribing to all events, as high-frequency events (state_changed with many sensors) can overflow slower consumers. Implement backpressure handling in custom WebSocket clients.

evidence_refs.description: WebSocket API documentation for Home Assistant

statement: Home Assistant integration with Loxone, Crestron, or KNX building automation systems uses the KNX integration or dedicated bridges to bring commercial building automation into HA.
